lines.st: 'Salt is sacred. It is within the tides, within our tears.'

lines.x: He strains his eyes in the dark waters, searching the lake bed for the faint glint of metal.
He slowly paddles forward, inch by inch, fingers outstretched in the silt, but all they find are strands of waterweeds.
The frigid waters flow past his skin and hair. Confusion sets in. He does not know how he will find that golden coin.

lines.st: 'Don't look with your eyes. Let the water's flow tell you where to go.'

lines.x: A voice from ashore cuts through the water. He cannot see the person's face clearly, but that gentle, friendly tone sounds reassuring.
And so he closes his eyes, feeling the water rush past him, and painting an image of the lake bed in his mind's eye.
There. A solid metal disc, embossed with a complex design, resting atop some wafting weeds.
He strokes its face before grasping it in his hand. He swims away, the stranger's face getting ever clearer as he gets closer to shore.

lines.st: 'Did you find the coin, my boy?'

lines.x: He unfurls his fingers, the gold coin resting on his palm. The circle on its face is bisected by a straight line. Somehow it resembles some distant horizon, one he has yet to reach.
Urgh...
What's that smell... Finny... Salty...
Mm... hmph...
*Sigh*...
(Sticks out tongue) Bleh...
Don't lick my face, little guy, or I'll hit you.
Mmph...
Forget it. I won't have to deal with you much longer.
Hey, chaval, your food. Eat up quick! Boss wants to see you!
She won't be seeing me today.
Isidro pulls down the canvas hammock hung up in the room, wrapping it around the hard bread and half-full canister of water on the tray.
He then kicks open the chest by his side, revealing a tunnel below it.
He had been digging away at it for a full week.
The strange-looking animal crawls up and nips onto his pants. Isidro watches its saliva soak into the fabric and sighs.
I'm definitely not coming back here.
(Whistle) Javier, can I get that blade I left here last month?
Javier!
Eh? Oh... Sorry, Teach, wasn't listening. You here for the blade?
What else? Not your sweat-stained pecs, I'll tell you that much.
You all need to stop it with the jokes, Teach.
Oh, don't be like that. Seeing you sharpening knives in the early morn is pretty much part of the landscape by now.
Look, you've drawn in a crowd... Though I don't think they're here to get their knives polished.
(Leers) Hey there, Javier!
What a pain in the— Here. Your blade.
We don't have much metal left for repairs, so I used other things to fix it up. You'll have to make do with it for a while.
Shell and bone? You're a real dab hand.
Wanna test it?
Got anything tough I can chop up?
I did have a hunk of dry bread... but I just gave it to the kid locked in the shack.
Waste of a perfectly good loaf.
Hey, hey, move it already! Don't you lot have anything better to do in the morning? Stop blocking the damn way!
Rus?
Oh, it's you. And here I was, thinking it was the harsh glare of dawn in my eyes.
I don't have time for you and your nonsense. Quick, Javier, let's head inside and talk!
Sounds like it's not for my ears. I'll be off then, Javier.
Seems like the fun has ended... See you tomorrow, Javier!
(Quietly) Hmph...
You need to talk to Juana. She's gone mad! She's going to send us all to our deaths, and you're the only one in her crew who can keep a cool head!
And how is that new?
Over all these years, every single one of her decisions looked crazy at first, but turned out to be exactly what we needed, right?
Getting in touch with that caravan a decade ago was a huge gamble too, but here we are, making a good living selling antiques we dig out of the salt flat.
Nobody took her side back then. Everyone was too scared of those money-grubbing merchants leaking the location of our fleet to the Inquisition.
But Juana got them to see what the real value of the deal was, and made them an offer they couldn't refuse.
This is different, Javier. Totally different.
Doesn't matter, Rus. She's our boss, and I'd say she's earned our trust.
She wants us to go back out to sea. You really going to back her on this? You really think this is a way forward?
'Cause it's not. We're all just gonna end up in a watery grave.
Show a little faith. She found the compass, and a caster to fix it up. Everything's going to plan, no?
Even if you don't give a damn what happens outside your kitchen, you must've noticed the finshit you've been getting to cook with lately, yeah?
Look, here are the bills for the wood she's been buying up. All to fix those rotten old ships, all to head back out to that sea of hers.
You know how expensive wood is. All that coin could've made our lives much cozier. You wouldn't be slaving away every day pickling saltfin livers for garum, I can say that much.
Just go talk to her, afore she's done too much damage.
Been hearing some real nasty talk around town. Folks're getting restless.
Fine, I'll have a chat with her.
But don't forget, Rus. Without that 'loca' Juana, our grandparents, our parents, us...
None of us would've been able to live out here in the Salt Flat.
Strange...
Where did that cook go? The one always sitting outside.
Mierda...
Are they trying to take me back?
A man turns the corner, aggression apparent in his footsteps. Isidro quickly hides himself in a shadowy corner.
But the man walks right by him, swearing up a storm as he goes.
Idiots! You blasted idiots!
Just you wait and see, that puta's going to get us all killed!
Clear your head, Cesc. You've been eating that girl's herbs on the sly again.
My head's clear as day, it's yours I'm worried about!
I can't be the only one with a problem with how she's leading us, can I?
You're all just too yellow to say it. Cowards, the lot of you!
Vete a la mierda. If you're going to spew shit, do it at home.
But am I wrong, Teach?!
Don't tell me you trust that loca too?!
Have you been taken in by her saltdamned lies too, like Javier?!
Cesc...
Hey, cool it! He's just a little upset!
Say that again.
Say. That. Again.
You know I'm right, Javier.
All those years of licking her boots. Shouting 'aye aye, Captain!' at all her shit.
We all know you're a loyal beckbeast.
So put down that knife of yours and go crying to Mamá like always.
You—!
Javier grabs Cesc by the collar and lifts him up, slamming his back into the wall.
Javier's face is calm, but the fire in his eyes burns bright, nearly bright enough to sear holes into the target of his ire.
Are they going to start a fight...?
If they do, I may as well make a break for it.
Urk...
Don't you ever spew that sort of finshit again. Not even in your sleep.
You hear me, Cesc?
Cesc can barely breathe as Javier presses into his neck. Nobody dares approach the two, only silently passing them with their heads lowered.
The people in the sheds lining the street shut their windows tight one after the other. In a flash, the street is empty.
Ugh...
Let him go, Javier! He's learned his lesson!
(Whispers) Not here, Javier...
ANSWER ME!
(Nods furiously)
*cough*... *cough* *cough* *cough*—!
Now run along home, chaval. Remember what you learned today. Remember it well.
*Sigh* The hell was that, Javier? You've never been that reckless.
Reckless? No. He was just making it clear to everyone where he stands.
Rus, I...
Relax, just bring him a little something tonight. He'll accept your apology.
Yeah. Thanks, Teach.
Javier watches the woman walk away as he stands in the middle of the street.
A stray headscarf lies at his feet, having fallen off Cesc's head as he ran.
Javier picks it up, pats off the dust, and walks back to his shed, closing the door lightly behind him.
Good, everyone's gone.
I was afraid I'd have to wait for them all to take an afternoon siesta.
Tch...
Why did I even try escaping at night last time? That's when these guys are most active.
(There. That's my way out. Just need to head down.)
(Only two guards. Gotta knock them out.)
Ahem...
Say, Anna... Ever thought about going someplace else?
I kept some of those old coins we've been digging up. File off the king's face, and you can barely tell the difference from the new ones!
We could leave the Flat behind. Go to Rim Billiton, Columbia... Anywhere you'd like.
No, José. I was born and raised here. Never even thought of leaving.
You really want to stay and follow the boss out to sea?
She's the boss.
The sea'll swallow you up...
I ain't afraid. She promised us.
Well, what scares me is that that promise might turn out to be a fleeting dream.
If you're that scared, then go. I ain't gonna stop you.
...I was kinda hoping you would.
(Are they done yet?)
If you don't wanna leave with me, then at least...
At least what?
(Can't this guy take a hint?)
Could you, uh, give me a...
(Stop mumbling and spit it out alr—)
Stop mumbling and spit it out already!
Unable to bear watching the man stumble over his words any longer, the woman knocks him flat before sandwiching his face between her hands, kissing him hard.
Isidro furrows his brows before looking away politely.
Mmmf... Anna...
Oh, just shut it!
(At least they didn't notice me.)
Phew...
Need to run faster... If I'm not out of here before dark...
Based on the last few times, I'm definitely going to be—
A giant helm wheel rolls past his face before coming to a halt, stopping just short of crushing his feet.
You shouldn't keep a lady waiting, you know.
Now be a good boy and come back with me.
You shouldn't be here.
Still not given up after all these attempts? We might not have enough spare medicine to patch you up if you end up wounded again, you know.
How did you know I was gone, Señora Juana?
I made sure nobody was following me.
It's my job to be aware of what's happening in my fleet. You can't hide from my little eyes and ears.
Besides, did no one ever teach you not to ask a lady a question she'd rather not answer?
The young man does not reply, silently thinking of the sword that had been faithfully at his side for years. If he still had it, perhaps it would even the odds.
But it had shattered. Just like the freedom currently within arm's reach, it had died in these salt flats, snapped in two.
Isidro clenches his fist, grits his teeth, and grabs onto whatever is left of his things.
The lady lightly draws her sword, her stance relaxed.
Out of my way!
Ngh—
After a few sword strokes, the unarmed man falls defeated, face in the salty sand. He could not protect even the last of his belongings.
As Juana ties him up and heaves him over her shoulder, he feels something in his heart silently shatter.
Ah, youth. Never knowing when to give up. No matter how many times you're beaten down, you're already dreaming of the next attempt.
If you're that desperate to leave, then fix that compass for me.
How many times I have to say it? Only an alchemist can fix it, and I'm no alchemist!
Oh? Then care to explain where that gold coin of yours came from?
...I stole it.
You are a terrible liar, joven.
On its face is a mark only a few know of: the mark of the long-lost school of alchemy. The knowledge they passed down helped countless fleets find treasure in the seas.
But after the Profound Silence, their disciples were imprisoned, and their works destroyed.
So tell me, why are you holding onto that coin, despite all that happened to them?
I don't know anything about any of that.
Oh, what a stubborn young man. How about we change the question, then?
How did you dig that tunnel of yours?
...The bread you gave me was really hard.
What a clever boy.
My oh my, I really must thank you!
You've worked so hard keeping watch over him. Why, if you hadn't informed me right away, he might have actually gotten away.
Yes, I know, he's not the best-behaved prisoner, but that's why we have you, isn't it?
You really are our cleverest, sharpest-witted, most amazing little...
Nene.
Mm-ph!
(Spins excitedly)
Don't get too excited now. There are still many difficult duties ahead of you, and you mustn't take them lightly.
(Quickly falls flat on the ground)
I have faith in you, of course. Alright, off you go now. Don't let that boy learn who you are.
Mmph!
Come in, Javi.
Pardon me, Señora Juana. I hope I'm not interrupting your time with Nene.
Mmph...
Don't give me that look. I've got a little something for you too.
My, what a fine little ball of bone... Did you carve it?
Sí. Out of bone shards that broke off some of our older weapons. Else they'd have gone to waste.
I wanted to give it to Rus as an apology, but he won't open his door for me.
Oh... I did hear about that. You shouldn't be so hasty, Javier. As our fleet's bosun, Rus has every right to question my plan to go out to sea.
And you attacking one of his men right in front of him can be seen as a rather harsh warning.
Considering your recent promotion to main chef, best keep a low profile. Some things are better left unsaid.
I think he's the one who needed that advice.
Just let him be, Javi. I don't pay it any mind, so why should you?
Because I know our boss isn't a madwoman playing around with other people's lives.
Well, aren't you sweet?
If we do get that compass fixed, can it really tell us how to avoid the sea monsters and find what we need?
Of course. I would never lie to you, Javi.
The Salt Flat won't be able to support us for generations to come. While the sea is a nigh inexhaustible font of resources.
Now that we have both the 'compass' and an alchemist to fix it, this is a chance for us to turn our lives around. We can't afford to miss it.
But aside from you, none of us have even seen the sea, or experienced that age of gold and plenty.
I've been living in the Flat for as long as I can remember, but the old cook said that only a century ago, this was fertile soil.
What if that bountiful sea of yours has already become just as barren as the Flat after the Profound Silence?
And this magic compass of yours... can't guide us back to the past.
'The tides will wash away my footprints, and the wind will scrub away the foam from the waves.'
'But the sea and the shore will stand, forevermore.'
What...?
Just an old poem, Javi. Unlike the land, the sea is tenacious. Though it may be infested with strangely fleshed beasts, it is still abundant with precious lifelines, waiting for us to find them.
It was once ours, and it can be ours once again.
I want to believe you, I do.
But these rumors... You have to prove yourself in front of everyone, show them you're not talking nonsense.
I will.
Oh, right. I did as you asked and went by the shed. There really is a hole in there. I'll fill it up tonight.
What should we do about the boy? He can't stay there anymore.
Don't worry, I've already found a place for him.
But where? If you mean my place, then no.
Don't you worry your little head over it. You'll see on your way home.
Damned little brat, this peg-leg you sold me is pure garbage! Two days and it's already split in twain!
Eh, it just needs a bit of spit, polish, and glue and it'll be good as new.
You sack of finshit, how about I rip your leg off and use that instead?!
H-Hey, settle down now! I'll take the leg back, no need to get violent!
I-I-I've got a knife! Don't come any closer!
Javier! Right on time! Help me catch the little wench!
Out of my way, idiot!
Tch... Troublemaker.
Ow! That hurts!
Give him back his money.
Let me go! I'll tell Juana you've been bullying me!
Use your head, chica. This isn't somewhere you can run your mouth. We're not like those cowards in other towns.
If you mess with us, we won't bother locking you up or putting you on trial. Left hand or right hand? Choose the one you want to keep.
H-How dare you! If it weren't for me, your boss would never have gotten ahold of that compass!
Care to try your luck?
Err... Fine, I'll return the money! But you gotta give me back that peg-leg!
Not on my life. If it's stuck to my leg, it's mine.
Y-You're no better than a pack of bandits!
I'm glad you've finally come to an accurate assessment of us, señorita.
Haha... Slip of the tongue is all... Here, the money. And, uh, adios.
What was the boss thinking, letting a dastardly little girl like that aboard our crew?
She's got her reasons.
I heard that kid ran off again. Why not just break his legs and be done with it?
And now she's gone and strung him up here, ordering us not to touch a hair on his head. Seeing that grumpy face of his pisses me right off. You don't know how much I wanna give it a good punch.
Hey, don't go, Javier! I was just thinking out loud!
Hey!
You've been running around all day. Want some water?
Cloudbeast got your tongue?
I won't surrender to you lot.
If that's how you want it, chaval.
Just here to give you the bad news. I'm afraid you're going to be out here like this all night.
...Whatever. I don't care.
Good to hear.
The dawn light falls upon the coin, and a stray ray reflects onto Isidro's eyelid, causing him to blink. The light wanders, and the circular marking on the coin begins to ripple.
He begins to feel discomfort and hands the coin to the man ashore, though the man is in no hurry to receive it. Instead, he grabs onto Isidro's legs and lifts him up by the feet.
The boy struggles, and the man ashore begins to speak:

lines.st: 'If you have nowhere to go, my young friend, you can come with me.'
'They call me Aulus, and you have my thanks for finding my coin.'

lines.x: The rays of the setting sun seep through the cracks of the salt ship's frame, shining onto Isidro's eyelid. He blinks in discomfort.
People walk by, and none can resist snickering at him as they pass.
He feels a little dizzy. The faint laughter causes his cheeks to flush, though it might just be an effect of being hung upside down.
Out here in the Salt Flat, simple tents and ramshackle shacks are put up one after the other.
Enormous salt ships are berthed on either side of the road, like a herd of slumbering seabeasts.
Upon their masts hang great sacks for gathering rainwater, flapping in the wind as they await clouds to pass overhead.
Upon the road, people walk shoulder to shoulder, laughing and squabbling, until all of a sudden a quarrel breaks out, which soon escalates into a chaotic all-out brawl.
And in the quieter corners, some are pulling their lovers in close for an intimate kiss.
These guys really are a bunch of barbarians.
Why'd I'd even come to such a lousy place?
Good question, Pascuala. How did I end up in this den of thieves?
*Cough* *Cough*
Not too sure myself...

eps.name: Upside Down on the Pale Sea

lines.st: Two weeks ago

lines.x: Beginning recording. My sandboard is heavily damaged, and I can't find the materials I need to repair it nearby.
As for my water reserves... They can last me one more day, at most.
Thankfully, the next supply point is only half a day away.
...This many messages? Really?
How long have I been letting them build up?
It's been a while since we received any news from you, Operator Thorns. Remember to report your position when you have the chance.
By my calculations, our mission routes will overlap a bit. If we can find the opportunity to meet up, I've got some spare supplies I can share.
Hey Thorns, how's your trip to find super-ultra-cool sword techniques going? Any good news?
Forget it... I'll reply once I'm done walking.
This region between me and the supply point... All the map shows is a name.
'Mar Pálido'... The Pale Sea, huh?
Hopefully it's not a real sea.
Out in the open wilderness, sand rides along with the wind, bringing with it a salty, finny smell.
Sunlight pokes through gaps in the dark clouds, while long-dry riverbeds boast only a few bushes and shrubs.
Some rustling among them proves that this so-called wasteland is not completely devoid of life.
The young man pulls out a rope and ties together a few planks. Once done, he steps atop them and takes a leap.
All of a sudden, the wastes come to an end. A great cliff has sundered the earth.
As he looks down, Isidro sees a pallid white sea.
With all his strength, he climbs down the cliff. Once he touches down onto the pale 'sea', he reaches down and sticks a hand into the bone-dry 'seawater'.
...Salt crystals?
There is no water here. There is only pale salt, studded with countless skeletons. The remains of whatever unlucky creatures were swallowed up by this place.
But the only thing on his mind is that somewhere, in some nook or cranny he cannot see, there is something flowing, rushing.
He stops sliding on his makeshift sandboard and reaches for the sword at his waist.
That's odd...
How can there be anything alive here?
The ground beneath his feet shudders slightly, and the layer of salt slowly subsides around him.
The trembling intensifies as an indentation in the salty ground approaches him at high speed.
There are fins in a Salt Flat?!
A gigantic beast breaches the salt, catching him off guard. Its sail-like dorsal fin looms overhead, and its pointed, armored snout is shaped like a colossal lance.
It barrels into him, and he is thrown off his feet. His organs feel like they are being rearranged as he arcs through the air, before he slams into the ground and blacks out.
As his consciousness fades, he sees the giant finbeast dive back into the salt, leaving no trace in its wake.
(Good thing... it wasn't hungry...)
*Cough* *Cough*... Just my luck...
That damn alguacil doesn't know when to give up. He even chased me all the way to this blasted land where nothing grows!
*cough* Mierda... Ran too fast, all my water spilled.
What now...
Urgh! Pleh! Bleh! This is just salt! Ptooey!
Without water, I'm not gonna make it to the town of Aarón...
Huh, what's that...?
A gust blows past a pile of salt and dust, revealing two motionless legs sticking out from beneath it.
...Just some other unlucky soul who wandered into the Salt Flat without knowing what he was getting into.
Your bad luck is my good fortune though, amigo. Hehe.

A full bottle of water! Excelente!
*Glug* *Glug*... *Glug*... Hah...
Let's see what else you've got...
The hell're all these bottles and canisters?
Urgh! What in the BLAZES is that smell?! It stings!
Well, whether it's medicine or poison, it should still fetch a pretty penny... Rhodes Island Pharmaceuticals? Whatever, mine now.
Huh? Foreign money? What, you expected find a money changer out here? Not even fowlbeasts shit here!
Buuuut... I might have a use for it later. Mine now.
The girl sifts through the man's wallet, finding only a few silver reales alongside some foreign coins and a meal voucher with 'Rhodes Island Cafeteria Soup Ticket' written on it.
At the bottom of the wallet, a glimmering gold coin catches her eye.
She reaches down to grab it, flipping it a few times as she retracts her hand.
On one side, there is a circle split by a horizontal line. On the other, an inverted triangle.
It's heavy... Must be pretty pure.
Wait, I feel like I've seen these patterns before... Aren't they what Juana's looking for...?
Shame. Why're you even lying out here dead anyway?
Well, you've been so generous, I can at least send you off on your final journey, amigo.
Here, have a proper burial.
A thief is nothing but a body hollowed out by greed. They take from others for their own benefit, but satisfaction ever eludes their grasp.
The moment she hears a sword being drawn, she knows it is too late to run. She looks behind her, her body trembling as her muscles refuse to move an inch.
A cleric of the Inquisition walks towards her, his handcannon and rapier decorated with ghastly bleached bones, a deep contrast to the usual solemnity and grace of his colleagues.
All in Iberia know the sheriff's name, but also know how close to madness his mind runs. None dare call him by it to his face. Behind his back, they only ever call him by his title:
'The Alguacil'.
The unreasonable, unfeeling, uncaring Alguacil. The Alguacil that inlays his weapons with the bones of criminals as a warning.
The Alguacil that will chase a criminal to the ends of Terra and back.
I have come to free you.
W—Wait a sec, this is all a big misunderstanding!
My head hurts... And my mouth's full of salt...
No! I don't wanna die! Mercy!
Hmm...?
Aaahh! Save me!
What's going on?!
Why do you struggle? Your death will free you from the eternal torment of sin.
O Most Holy Señor Anastasio, please let me go! See reason, please!
Someone forced me to do it, really! I... I swear on my life! I don't even have it anymore, honest!
If you don't believe me, search me! I don't have any compass! I-If you let me go, I'll take you to them!
If others may impose their greed upon you, then so too will your greed corrupt others.
All who have been tainted by avarice shall receive judgment.
AHHH! Someone, save me! Anybody!
The Alguacil's rapier sweeps across her face, but before it presses against her neck, a black sword swoops in from behind, knocking aside its point.
A golden liquid drips from the black sword's edge, sizzling as it eats away at the salt on the ground, an acrid scent spreading from the splash.
Run.
Th-Th-The dead guy's alive!
I am currently arresting a criminal who has stolen a forbidden relic from the Inquisition, señor. I did not mean to disturb you during your repose.
You're with the Inquisition?
Before you shield this vermin from justice, I pray you first ascertain if all your belongings are still on you.
What?
My wallet...
Your swordsmanship is impeccable. It should be used to shelter the innocent, not harbor criminals.
Geh... Mierda.
...What is this?
Isidro pats down his body. In the depths of his pocket, he feels something small yet hard.
An irregularly-shaped piece of metal. At first glance, it looks like detritus from a factory, but careful examination reveals the fine alchemical symbols etched all across it.
The layers of rust on it appear to have once been some kind of liquid, but even so, it shows a gleaming luster under the sunlight.
And it seems we have discovered where Señorita Pascuala hid her ill-gotten gains. Hand it to me, señor, then leave in all haste.
This is... a 'compass' filled with Corazonix? I thought all the products of alchemy were destroyed.
Oho... And here I thought that none sinless in Iberia would yet recognize it.
The Alguacil turns his rapier toward Isidro, his severe expression shifting towards quizzical.
You have piqued my curiosity, señor. What relation have you with the wanted criminal Aulus? Or rather, where was it you came into contact with him?
I have so many questions for you, Ægir.
How is it you can recognize a sinful product of alchemy with but a single glance? How is it you know of a long-erased school of alchemy?
...It's none of your business, Alguacil.
Oh, but it very much is. You see, most of that discipline's students have died by my blade.
Isidro considers saying something, but the Alguacil's rapier is already pressing against his throat.
...So you are unwilling to submit to the law.
The Alguacil pulls back his sword as he shifts his center of gravity forward. The blade in his hand spins once before coming down in a brutal chop.
Isidro's intuition as a swordsman tells him that this man is a lunatic. Not even the most unorthodox of fighters would wield a rapier in such a crude manner.
The cold gleam of the blade's edge flashes past his eyes. With an instinctual flick of his wrist, he blocks the savage blow.
A rapier is not made for slicing. Isidro laughs inwardly. Brandishing it like that will only cause it to snap.
But then comes a brittle screech of shattering metal, and he sees it is his own sword that has snapped in two.
Half his fractured sword is stuck point-down into the salty soil. Blood drips down his cheek.
Lay down your arms.
Run!
The Alguacil casually brushes aside the rock the girl threw at him. The second he does so, Isidro runs off to gain some distance.
As he runs, he senses familiar vibrations coursing through the layer of salt underfoot. The pain in his jumbled organs crawls up his nerves once more.

The Alguacil's footsteps approach him as the chase continues. Isidro's mind races. He grits his teeth, and without looking back, he sets his sights on a giant skeleton in the distance poking out of the salt.
Hah, none may leave before I have the truth of the matter.
Do you always talk this much?
(Just... a little... faster...)
Hah...
As he runs from the man hounding him, Isidro holds his broken sword high, doing his utmost to fend off the Alguacil's attacks.
He looks behind his pursuer and sees the layer of salt subside, a familiar furrow appearing on the ground once more.
The Alguacil stops. He too has sensed the vibrations.
An earthquake?!
Looks like this is your first time here too.
Before the words have finished leaving his mouth, the colossal finbeast breaches the ground in front of him, sending its prey flying.
As he flies through the air, the man's limbs splay out like a ragdoll. After a moment of soaring, his graceful arc through the sky comes to an end as he meets the ground.
A puff of salty dirt wafts through the air as the sound of his landing echoes through the dry air.
He has not fallen on the most comfortable spot in the Salt Flat. A sharp bone spur has pierced his body. His blood trickles down the skeleton, staining the pale salt below red.
Phew...
Is... Is he dead?
If he isn't, we're in a whole lot of trouble. If he is, we're in a whole lot of trouble later.
Err... What do you mean, señor?
Did you put this thing in my pocket?
It just fell in as I was pulling you out of that salt pile, honest!
Ahaha... Er, do you believe me?
My pockets have zippers.
Ah, then allow me to beg forgiveness for my transgressions, señor. Still, we're both alive, right?
Why didn't you run when that alguacil was attacking me?
Would you believe me if I said I found a conscience?
Look, if you died too fast, I wouldn't be able to get very far anyway... Not to mention you still had the compass. The buyer paid a loooot of money for it.
If I did try to book it, all that hard work would've been for nothing.
Where are you headed?
Huh?
You might have been the source of my problems, but you also helped me out, so I'll return the favor.
I'll get you where you need to go, and then we're even.
Huh? 'Then we're even'... You haven't even considered going on an adventure with me?
Or we could part ways here. What you stole from me should be enough to keep you going for a while.
I... I'm heading to the town of Aarón, up on that hill over there, but it's a good two days walk from here...
Two days? Half a day should be plenty.
Huh? Really?
The girl senses the underground vibrations closing in on them once more. A towering dorsal fin breaks through the salt and sand, rapidly approaching them.
She gawks at him incredulously, but he looks back with steely certainty.
Mind if I borrow your rope? My sandboard's broken.
Stop hitting it so much, you'll crack the wood.
I wanted to make it sturdier.
...It's sturdy enough already. You were the one who fixed it up in the first place. Unsatisfied with your own work?
Señor Silver said this is how I should do my work, if I want to repent.
What's the sin this time?
The widow looks like she's about to give birth, but she won't let me look after her.
*sigh* She must still be haunted by my past sins that caused my wife's death. This is divine retribution.
I think she just doesn't want to remarry.
What are you talking about? It's been two years since this town has seen its last birth. I have to help take care of the child, no? I would never have such ulterior motives...
That's a good point. Most of the people young enough to have kids have already gone. All that's left here are us old timers.
Wait here and—
W—Wait! Over there! Santo cielo, the sky's full of salt!
Saltfins? Or is it a saltstorm?
Doesn't matter, just run! Aaaaaaaahhhhhhhhh!!!
The cloud of salt and dust swallows up the fleeing townsfolk as they scream in fear.

Soon after, a colossal saltfin parts the cloud, revealing two people riding on its back, one in front of the other. The young man in front pats the saltfin as he extracts a broken sword from its body.
The saltfin digs into the salt crust with its pectoral fins, giving the man a resentful glare before it departs.
He pays no mind to it, instead meeting the townsfolk's fearful gazes as he approaches them.
Sorry to trouble you, señores, but is this the town of Aarón?
Who are you? What do you want?
Isidro. I'm here to find something. Here, my travel pass.
Travel pass...?
(Quietly) Didn't Señor Silver say that nowadays there's a lot of Ægir coming ashore to help us rebuild?
(Quietly) Does he look like he's here to help?
(Quietly) All these outlanders bring with them is disaster!
If I'm to die today, then let me die blocking your way!
Huh? What are you talking about, señor?
H—Hey, let's all calm down now. This is a misunderstanding.
See here? This is my alguacil's badge. The higher-ups sent me and my understudy here to meet the local calificador.
Look, it's real. It looks just like Señor Silver's.
You... He's... He's the student?
He... He had a growth spurt.
Can... Can Ægir even join the Inquisition?
...It's all part of the plans for restoration.
*Sigh*...
I simply cannot keep up with these changing times.
Uh... Thanks. Did you swipe that badge off that guy earlier?
Just asking, not judging. You don't look so good.
I can't... hold it... any longer...
Blurghhh—!
Oh...

I'm just... a bit—
Bleaghhh—!
Saltfin-sick...
Blurghhh—!
Between bursts of vomit, the girl stuffs the badge back into her bag.
A wayward glance shows Isidro a dozen or so of the Alguacil's belongings within bag, alongside a few bottles of his own popper potions.
He is not entirely sure when she took them off him.
...Forget it.
We're even now. I'll be off, then.
You're asking the wrong guy.
I haven't even gotten to the question yet...
Go ask somewhere else.
I haven't even...
Ask someone else.
At least let me ask! I heard there's a local sword technique created by pirates, one that can be used to 'fight the wind and waves'. Does that sound familiar?
P—Pirates? Santo cielo, Aarón will be ruined once more...
Wha...?
It's about to rain! I need to go home to take in the laundry! Just go away! Vete!
...Isn't this your house right here?
...Also, isn't this the driest part of Iberia?
As he voices his doubts, he hears a clattering of footsteps behind him. He turns to see the entire street devoid of people.
As he sweeps his eyes across the street, doors and windows slam shut one after the other in raucous cacophony.
Is it actually going to rain?

lines.st: The lump of meat in his mouth is shriveled and dry. One of his left molars is loose and hurts like hell itself, leaving him only able to mash the meat with his right gums.
Madre had told him this was the last of the food in the house.
He thinks about how she looked as she threw it on the ground, so tender and caring. He thinks about how she looked as he picked it up, so cold and cruel.
Such contradiction. He blames the jumbled memories on the hunger.
Just as he does not remember if it was Madre who struck that molar loose, or if it was just a new one coming in.
The hunger is too great. He swallows the lump of meat and mumbles to himself.
'This is the last portion. There's none left after I eat it...'
'Where can I find more...'
There is the sound of a knife being sharpened from outside the door. He remembers hearing this noise... before Padre disappeared.
The ghastly truth dawns on him as he feels cold all over.
He recalls how Madre opened the door as she held the meat, a terrifying smile on her bloodstained face. She bade him open his mouth, bade him chew, bade him swallow...
He licks at the empty sockets in his gums. They had hurt, once. They don't hurt any more.
It is only because of sin that he yet lives. He should stretch out his neck and await the axe. In death, all things will be washed away.

lines.x: The scent of dried blood upon a sharp bone spur has drawn all sorts of small, bloodthirsty beasts. They cannot stop themselves from crawling towards the gruesome wound.
First come insects bearing wings, landing upon his chin, crawling across his pale lips, then his cheek, before finally stopping atop his eyelids.
Just as they are about to enjoy their meal, the man's eyelashes begin to twitch.
Ah...
Anastasio, you sinner. Since when was it your time to die?
Those ensnared by greed, corrupted by that compass. They yet roam free, yet commit evil.
You must find them, purge their avarice, end their suffering.
...This is your duty.
The man slowly pulls himself off the skeleton, the bone barbs and hooks tearing at his flesh as he does.
But he does not tremble in pain. Not even once.
Anastasio walks deeper into the Salt Flat. Behind him, the beasts hungrily paw at his trail of blood.

eps.name: Upside Down on the Pale Sea

lines.x: Pascuala! You made it. Here I was, thinking you were buried in the Flat.
*Huff*...
You're as pale as the salt. Run into something on the way?
Shut up, Teach... Let me in already.
You wanna come in? Did you get what the boss asked for?
'Course I did. Not just the compass, either. I got another big haul too.
Señora Juana's been looking for an alchemist, and I've got a lead.
So can I come in now?
Of course, Master Thief Pascuala. You honor my humble abode.
Anchors aweigh, hoist the sails♪
High winds roar, our clothes a-tore♪
Rain drowns our drunken haze, and lights our hearts ablaze♪
Ready, ho! Sail forth! A thousand ships break port!♪

At dawn break we charge ahead♪...
Aaaahhhh! Who are you?! What're you doing in my house?!
This alley... is your house, señor?
That cardboard you've got on your head is my bed, joven...
Sorry, I saw it by the garbage bin. Didn't think anyone wanted it.
Mind if I hide from the rain here? I'll go once it stops.
This town hasn't seen a drop in over two months.
Even if it is going to rain, mind putting down my bed? Here, you can use my blanket.
...Thank you, señor.
So what're you here for, Ægir?
I'm looking for a local sword technique. The legends say the ones who devised it were—
Ahem. A few months ago, I heard about a technique that can 'fight the wind and waves', and decided to come here to learn it.
That's mighty brave of you. Don't you know the Inquisition's been cracking down on pirates these days?
I'm not interested in becoming a pirate. I just feel like my own swordsmanship's been lacking.
Swords are for cutting people down, not cutting through the waves. Besides, why'd you want to learn it anyway? Heading out to sea?
Too many questions, señor.
*Sigh* There's always folks believing such foolishness. If you'd come by half a year earlier, I could have brought you to the guy you're looking for.

Back in the day, he learned a thing or two from those pirates, always bragging about how he could chop up the sea itself. In the end, he was just like me. Came running back to town, waiting to die in peace and quiet.
Did he leave anything behind?
His diary? Took that to his grave. You'll have to dig it up yourself.
If he knew someone wanted to learn the sword from him, he'd be so happy he'd jump right out, bones and all.
With a shovel by his side, Isidro cracks open the coffin. He brushes aside the grains of salt that fell inside, exposing a lustrous, red-flushed corpse.
The corner of a book pokes out of its clothes.
Isidro gingerly pinches the book and pulls it away, doing everything he can to keep the flimsy paper—made brittle by the salt—intact.
Is this the diary he mentioned?
It's too tattered, I can't read a thing.
*Cough* *Cough* *Cough*... Pfft—pleh!
Did I just inhale bits of paper...? Damn, that's salty.
I should look elsewhere.
Elsewhere? Where else would a wicked grave robber and cannibal such as yourself want to spread his evil?
Grave robber? Cannibal???
I've been watching you creep about the cemetery, molesting its graves. I've caught you red-handed.
I can explain, señor.
'Señor'...? Do you not know who I am?!
Do you... happen to be a local celebrity?
I am a cleric of the Inquisition, in charge of inspecting the entry permits of all visitors at the town gates.
So if you don't know who I am, then how did you get in here?
Guards! Arrest this man!
Are these petals crushed enough...?
Doctor Añora!
What? Oh! More or less, a few more times and you can start blending in the oil.
Why are your hands shaking?
You must be mistaken.
Taken? What was taken?
I said! You must be mistaken!!!
About what? What are you on about? Still, that much shaking... Have you been drinking again?
You're the town calificador, you shouldn't be drinking. Wine is sinful and brings only harm, you said so yourself.
I may have had a few cups with the representative of the wine caravan as we talked business, but it was all for the sake of securing the town's future.
How is the widow doing, Doctor?
The who now? Speak up!
The potter's widow! She's giving birth in a few days!
Gaining girth? And now gluttony, yet more sin.

I said!
The widow!!
Is giving birth!!!
Señor Silver—!
Ah—! Dios mio, you scared me. Didn't I say to knock before you enter?
I heard you shouting and assumed you were in danger!
I'm fine.
I, uh, aside from that... I have something to report.
We've captured a... a lunatic, a blasphemer in the cemetery east of town. He was stealing corpses in the cemetery.
What?!
I... I thought that with enough food, we could avoid such a tragedy happening again.
If I may, Señor Silver, a full belly is not enough.
When you first brought the holy Law to our town, you cleansed us of our sins. But now? Not only have your prohibitions been lifted, we have not heard your sermons in a very long time.
Sin flourishes once more. It is inescapable.
Is that what you think?
Which is precisely why you cannot allow this vile blasphemer to spread his evil in town!
...I shall arrange a sentencing.
What of Old Palo? He was seen talking to the blasphemer, for quite a while at that.
...No need. Palo may have associated with pirates in his youth, but he wouldn't have the nerve to break the law.
But—
I said there is no need.
Sir... I believe we should at least keep an eye on him.
We can discuss this later.
Digging up a grave? Don't tell me he was about to... you know...?
Sinner! A sinner! Aarón is no longer pure... Calamity comes once more...
Padre, Calificador! I beg you, kill him! Cleanse his sins! Don't let us be visited by disaster again!
(Struggles)
Stop that!
Señor Isidro, is it true you were stealing from the graves of this town's residents in the middle of the night?
I don't deny being at the cemetery, but I—
What?!
Pascuala?
Isidro, amigo, I never took you for a grave robber. Of all the things to steal, corpses? Really?
Makes me right sick, it does.
Might I remind you, Pascuala, that you're chained up too.
Silence, sinner! You'll be on trial next!
What are you even doing here?
I... *sigh* Old habits.
She's about to be executed?!
Don't blame me, that girl ran afoul of her bad habits. Barely out the door and she was already off to pick the pocket of the best dressed man in town.
Who happened to be Calificador Silver.
With her skills, not even the calificador would have noticed her.
He didn't, but she was caught in the act by one of his priests giving a report.
And what about that alchemist she mentioned?
We know where he is.
That's good.
He's about to be hanged, right alongside her.
So, how many of those priests do they have at the execution?
Twelve.
...With your crew, we should be able to handle them. Let's go.

Señoras y señores, according to the laws of this town, these two criminals will be hanged by the neck until dead. I hope this will serve as a warning to all not to allow your souls to be tarnished by sin.
The time has come. Execute them.
You don't look worried at all.
What's there to worry about?
You got a way out of this then?
Not at this exact moment, no, but it's all about the timing...
The girl turns her head, looking to the side of the gallows. A warrior priest has his hand on the lever, ready to pull it.
(Waves his hand)
Agh! My hand!
B—Bandits! Bandits!
OhwhatsinshaveIcommittedtodeservesuchpunishmenthowshouldIatone...
Repel them! Protect the civilians! Cover Calificador Silver's retreat!
Forget me! Evacuate the citizens!
Charge, you lazy sons of saltfins, charge! Don't let those poor bastards bite the dust!
Yearrrgh!
Those bandits are here to rescue their friends!
Get in formation and stop them!
Die!
Oh...
You dare plunder this town so brazenly?!
We'll handle this lot, Javier! You save Pascuala and the boy, quicklike!
I owe you one.
Don't let them have their way!
Yes, sir!
Keep it quick! There's no time to waste!
Anyone gets in our way, cut them down!
Aye!
Grkh...
Cut them loose, quick!
On it!
Heh, took you guys long enough. Quick, get me out of these shackles, it's starting to chafe like you wouldn't believe.
Yeow! My wrists! You call this a rescue?
Hmph. Like I told the boss long ago, you're nothing but trouble.
And yet here you are, saving me from the gallows.
You too, chaval. Get moving.
Can I borrow a sword?
Here, catch.
Don't even think about running, boy!
You expect me to lay down and die, señor?
Nice moves!
Muchas gracias.
Hey! Is this really the time to get all buddy-buddy?! How're we getting out of here?
Shut your mouth and follow Teach. She knows an alley leading out of town.
An alley? I guess that'll do...
Follow me!
They're heading south!
South...? It's a sheer cliff. What're they up to?
*Huff*... *Huff*... You're nuts. What alley?! That's a CLIFF!
There's an alley if you jump down.
Please tell me she's joking, Javier.
Run faster! You'll need the speed to make the jump!
Huh?
(Silently speeds up)
Don't tell me you're going along with this plan?
Any better ideas?
I think you've got brain problems, amigo.
It's just ahead! Three, two, one—JUMP!
With a decisive leap, the bandit jumps down the cliff, knocking loose some rocks that fall alongside her. After a long silence, a distant, muffled 'thump' can be heard coming from the bottom.
Pascuala watches bandit and boulder alike disappear off the cliff's edge, and gulps in fear.
Urk...
It's a dead end up ahead, there's nowhere to run! Get them!
Jump already! They're about to catch up!
Pascuala looks at the cliff, then back at the warrior priests pursuing them.
No! I'd rather get cut down than jump!
Zip it.
Hey! Wh—What are you doing?!
With the girl tucked snugly under his arm, Isidro takes one last look back.
Then he sets his sights firmly ahead and leaps down the cliff, a screaming Pascuala in tow.
AHHHHHHHHH!
You lousy piece of finshit, I'll remember this—!
AAAAAAAAHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHHH!
In the midst of her panic, Pascuala feels herself land on something soft, quite unlike the solid ground she expected.
As she opens her eyes, she sees the giant cloth used to catch her.
We're safe now. They caught us with their sails.
Oh, there was... a boat... at the bottom of the cliff.
She looks around. Enormous bones prop up the ship's wooden hull, while layer upon layer of canvas and finleather sails throng the masts, scattering the sun's light.
The fiery wind powers the complex of legs at the bottom of the hull through a complicated transmission system, the legs sending the ship scuttling over the salty soil with their powerful strokes.
Still calling yourself a Master Thief after that courageous display, Pascuala?
Hah, a good thief acts with thorough planning and caution, not reckless derring-do.
Stop gabbing with her and get changed, Teach. The helmsman's injured and I need you to take over.
Didn't like that outfit anyway. Way too tight around the bodice.
*Huff*... *Huff*...
Hm? What's that you're carrying?
Food and water!
I spotted their granary earlier, so I snagged some in the chaos!
Set it alight afore I left, too! Haha! They'll be too busy putting out the fire to worry about us.
Not half bad, you bastard.
Now put your backs into it, ladies and gents! We reach home tomorrow night!
What're you looking at, chaval? You'll fall over the railing like that. First time in Mar Pálido?
This ship is... unusual. It's almost entirely wood and bone... Must be a lot lighter than the average landship.
Of course. Our salt ships are the only thing that can cross the Flat. If you tried it with those heavy iron beasts, they'd just get stuck in the salt.
Fascinating.
You don't look the least bit scared. Didn't you hear what the townsfolk called us?
Bandits.
You've got guts, saying it straight to my face.
I mean, you saved me from the gallows, didn't you?
Still, why run with us? The townies will brand you as one of us now.
...Do you know of a sword technique that will let you cut through the sea's wind and waves?
A sword technique? You having a laugh?
So none of you know?
Never heard of it.
Where's your ship stopping?
You want off? No can do, I'm afraid. You're heading back with us.
...Sorry, but that's not in my plans.
Not my problem, chaval.
Don't say that. I'd rather not have to draw my sword against the ones who saved me.
I get that you're a dab hand with the blade, but...
Grkh...
You need to watch your back.
Heh, all these years as a thief, and not once has anyone spotted me as I make my move.
That was enough dizzyleaf smoke to keep him asleep the entire trip.
Don't get cocky. So where's the compass the boss wanted? We put a lot of work into saving your sorry behinds, so don't disappoint us now.
You wound me, señor. I've just delivered you more than just the compass.
(Pouts) I got you my amigo over there too, didn't I?
In fact, considering the added value, saving me's not enough. I'll need a little something extra to make it worth my while.
You think we're just gonna take your word for it that he's an alchemist? Wait til the boss checks him over afore you run your mouth again.
Fine by me. Whatever's meant to be in my pocket, will find its way there eventually.
Hmph.
Anyhow, where's the kitchen? I'm always a little peckish after staring death in the face.
Second floor of the stern.
Muchas gracias, O generous señor!
AHHHHHHHHH!
AAAAHHHHH—!
I'm only going to remove the bone spines from the wound, not amputate your leg.
Only the most vicious of bandits would make barbed blades like this... The bleeding's stopped.
Urgh...
Hold on a little while longer. I've already sent someone to get Doctor Añora. Once she's here, we can have a better look.
Be at ease, you seem fine. You'll be better after a few square meals.
Señor Calificador, we've put out the fire in the granary, but there's nothing left inside.
...I'll think of a way to feed us.
What of the doctor? Her legs are not so good at her age. Did you not help her over?
She's gone.
Then where is sh—Wait, what? What happened?!
She was knocked over by the bandits and hit the back of her head. She is... no longer with us.
...I see.
Señor Silver... Are you...?
Did that hurt...? Sorry... Bear with it.
She's giving birth, Silver! The widow's giving birth right now!
Might be because of the bandits' attack. Or not. Can we have the doctor see to her first?
...Wait, where's the doctor?
Don't ask. I'll check on her myself.
Don't shake her. She won't be waking.
Señor, I... Señor, she... Señor, the child...
The child, too.
Señor, you...
Clerics, guard the bodies. We bury them tomorrow morning.
Sir.
Prepare a burdenbeast. After the funerals, go and search for Añora's son. We must ask him to return, no matter what.
This town needs a doctor.
As I recall, she was teaching you some of her craft, was she not?
A man who has only just learned how to make ointment cannot be considered a doctor.
Otherwise, the widow and her child would not have died.
Now go, back to work.
Through the tightly closed shutters of their windows, the people of Aarón can see a faint pink light floating through the fog-choked streets.
Silver does not know when it became dark. It feels like it was morning only minutes ago, when he was tidying up his robes for the execution.
But now, those robes are stained with blood. Not the blood of thieves or blasphemers, but of the townspeople.
Out of nowhere, he sees another faint pink light across the street.
A gaunt, lanky man with a bloody hole in his abdomen, large enough for him to see right through it.
The man calmly walks towards him, as if nothing was wrong.
Do you need assistance, señor?
You look gravely wounded.
I am fine, Señor Calificador. You on the other hand, look most pale indeed.
I am an alguacil, newly transferred. Might you require my assistance?
Ahh—
I must say, I didn't expect you to sleep so well hanging upside-down, joven.
This sort of thing happens to me a lot. You get used to it.
Your old shed's as good as new. Remember to thank Javier. It took him all night to fill in that tunnel of yours.
You could have saved him the effort if you just let me go.
Yet you're the one giving himself nothing but trouble by refusing to fix my compass. Do that, and you're free.
I told you, I don't know a thing about alchemy. That's not going to change, no matter how many times you ask.
Send him back to his shed. And skip the bread for his meals.
You're always looking out to sea when you're alone.
When I was but a wee babe, you'd pick me up, point out into the distance, and tell me that if I kept going that-a-ways, I'd see an ocean bluer than the sky above.
That was fifty years ago.
There's things that happened a hundred years ago you still haven't forgotten, señora.
Oh, my little perlita, if you had captained a ship across the churning waves like I once had, seen lightning crash into lightning between the clouds, heard the unending peals of thunder...
You'd never forget a moment of it your entire life either.
I'm mighty curious, though, what's this sea the old folks keep going on about really like?
I've lived in this Flat my whole life, so I don't get it at all, no matter how much I rack my brains.
Before my old man died, he'd talk day in day out about going out to sea, about how he followed you and Captain Cruz in filling your holds with treasure—so full your ships would be low in the water.
Your father was the nimblest topman I've ever seen. He'd climb masts like he was going for a stroll in a garden.
Shame there's not many sailors left who've actually seen the sea for themselves.
And that's my biggest worry, señora. Even with the compass leading the way, we lack seasoned sailors.
When it comes to the sea, Perlita, we are all reckless, ignorant children.
*Sigh* ...Nowadays, it's starting to feel like even heading out into the Flat's more trouble than it's worth.
A little fowlbeast told me that a particularly astounding fellow has come to Aarón, and in the process cut off nearly all our trade routes.
Have they finally decided to take us seriously?
He managed to sniff out all the sites we use to contact the caravans like some kinda beckbeast. Few decades ago, the useless bastards in town couldn't even spot our footprints.
*Sigh*... Stealing the compass out from under the noses of a massive garrison. To the Inquisition, it might as well have been both humiliation and provocation.
They'll do whatever they can to find us. It won't be easy.
We're running out of time, and the boy won't budge... What do we do, señora?
What if he's telling the truth? That he's not an alchemist?
I am absolutely certain he is one, Perlita.
The coin on him has the symbols for 'salt' and 'water' on its faces. A set of symbols particular to the school of alchemy. And that particular combination means 'look to the sea for life'.
I am familiar with the coin's former owner, Aulus. He was the most talented of the alchemists.
Moreover, the boy has the same style of swordplay as him. Even their speech has similarities as well.
Even if they knew each other, that doesn't mean he taught him anything. The coin could've been a gift.
No. If Aulus gave that to someone, it can only mean he had unusually high expectations for him.
According to their traditions, the coin can only be passed from teacher to student.
So why won't he admit it, then?
That puzzles me as well. I've already made it perfectly clear he has nothing to fear if he cooperates...
Young Isidro bites down on the root of his tooth with great force, but he cannot stem the flow of stinging pain bubbling up from his gum.
Before him, a former classmate's corpse lies under the foot of his mentor.
The corpse's long-cold limbs are lined with an azure mucus that makes him retch.
Through the tears in his eyes, a gentle voice like flowing water pierces the depths of his heart.
Why do you wipe away your tears, Isidro?
You killed him...
The sea and the land are as one. To mix them together is nothing more than erasing the barriers between them.
He decided, of his own free will, to embrace the salt and water flowing within his own body, to share all of his existence with us.
Be not afraid, be not hateful, derisive, loathing... Shut not the windows, bar not the doors, close not your heart. No longer shall there be polluted blood or shameful pain.
There will come a day when all will give their all to each other.
Suddenly, the corpse on the floor jerks like a fin out of water, letting out a gurgle of what sounds like grief, before falling flat once more.
Isidro watches the corpse's eyelids slowly lift, revealing the eyes of some unfamiliar creature.
Its lips open wide, and a voice, human yet inhuman, warbles out.
'You are... the same... as me...'
No—!
I will never... I will never be like you...
I won't...
*Sigh*...
Mm-ph!
(Flops about)
Hmm...?
(Claps fins together)
You...
(Nibbles ferociously)
Ow! What are you doing?!
Nene twists around his leg, trying to use its rotund body to cover his foot.
A variety of sounds leak into the shed from his surroundings. Some weeping, some arguing, but mostly cursing.
Isidro hopes they aren't cursing at him. Wait... He freezes in place as the gears in his subconscious mind begin to turn.
He sticks out his head. No guard outside his door. How unusual.
A line of people walk through the middle of the haven, their heads hung low, their clothes smeared with blood.
Those at the back of the line are dragging planks behind them, with people sporting heinous wounds lying atop them. The wounds are so deep their bones are visible, bandaged only with rags.
Their blood has soaked into the planks, leaving behind long red streaks on the white salt.
Only the occasional gasp stands as proof that they still live.
Only half of 'em made it back...
*Sigh*...
What... What happened out there?
The Inquisition capullos ambushed us at dawn on the only road out. A lot of our mates gave their lives to see us live... Took everything we had to escape.
The townsfolk don't have salt ships. How'd they get deep enough into the Flat to even find you?
Who knows?
They're like ghosts, like they don't feel pain. They don't even fall down if you cut them open. They just get right back to attacking you instead.
(Whispers) The clerics in town barely knew the blade from the hilt... How could they pull off something like this?
Oh, there you are, Teach. Where's the rest of your men? Managed to get done what needed doing?
Heavy losses, just like you. There's another crew I haven't heard a peep from either.
...If this goes on, we won't even have enough men to continue salvaging.
Teach is about to say something when she spots Isidro among the crowd.
She walks over, grabs him, and drags him to Rus.
How'd you get out?
No guard at the door.
Bring him with you.
Me?
Aye.
I say nay.
I'm saying nay too. This is your problem. I'm not getting involved.
If you want to eat, you have to work, chaval. If not alchemy, then you'll have to offer something else.
Why not just let me go already?
Don't even think about it. Best prepare yourself to spend the rest of your days here. We're sorely low on manpower.
...Stop pulling me.
Morning of the day after tomorrow, Rus, you bring the boy here. I'll go tell the boss.
What is even going on right now?
Mm-ph!
You want to come along too?
(Nods furiously)
No.
(Gnaws fin indignantly)
Put the luggage down here, Leaf.
Wait up, Weedy!
I don't understand... Why did you bring so much stuff with you?
I haven't seen Thorns in a while, right? As his best friend, it's only natural I have a few things to give him.
You could've let Leaf carry them.
Why bother, when this is barely any—
Mierda, the box broke open.
*Sigh* Help tidy this stuff up, Leaf.
What a big bag... Cleanser, lotion... Oof, that's heavy! Why so many skincare products?
How else am I supposed to protect myself from this scorching sun? I'm not having a repeat of the tragedy from my last holiday.
Holiday? Aren't we here to find Thorns after he went missing?
Bah, he's a big boy now, he can handle getting a little lost. Let's see, what's in this bag here... Oh right, clothes.
Do you really need an entire suitcase full of outfits for this mission?
As a representative of Rhodes Island, it's very important that I maintain a certain image.
Wait, so which of these were you giving to Thorns?
That package there, the one you're stepping on!
Sunscreen... Waterproof, cooling, whitening... Does he really need any of this?
What kind of friend would I be if I let him burn to a crisp?
...Well, you know him better, so if you say so.
Hey, careful with those, watch what you're throwing!
Someone's coming. We shouldn't block the street.
Will you be holding a sermon for us tonight, Señor Alguacil?
Also, I must thank you! My wife used to sneak into the kitchen every night to eat the grain we had stored away, while keeping to the teachings against gluttony by day.
But ever since hearing you preach, she hasn't sinned even once! Your teachings have cleansed her soul, and for that, I am grateful beyond words!
I merely shared with everyone all my worldly possessions...
You... You are far too humble, señor.
Señor Alguacil, please, allow me to hold your hand. Such frightful events have happened in Aarón lately, and I'm terribly afraid...
You may, if it might bring you comfort.
Wh—While I have never sinned, I was wondering if you could...
If you could lay your hand atop my head...
Alright, no crowding, one at a time!
Control yourselves! Think about your request before your turn comes! Do not take up too much of the Alguacil's time!
(Whispers) The locals here seem a little... odd...
(Whispers) Probably because they trust in the Inquisition.
(Whispers) Maybe...
I wonder who's in charge here.
May I help the two of you?
Ah, that would be amazing! What should I call you, amigo?
Anastasio is my name.
(Whispers) This dude's really popular...
(Whispers) That's for sure. Everyone who sees him stops to greet him.
Calificador Silver is waiting inside. Pray, enter.
Ah, the burden of those cursed with charm. I know it all too well, amigo.
...Pardon?
Is this man your...?
Oh, we're just colleagues. Honestly, I barely know him.
*Ahem* I am afraid we do not have what we need to hold a proper reception for you, my guests.
The town granary was recently burnt down by bandits, so I'm afraid we're all going a bit hungry before the next caravan arrives.
Don't worry about it, we brought our own food.
I am truly sorry for this.
I believe we have yet to formally introduce ourselves?
This is Field Operator Elysium, and I'm Engineering Operator Weedy, both from Rhodes Island Pharmaceuticals.
You're an engineer, then?
Yes, I am.
Are you familiar with the mechanics behind wind-powered light landships?
Well, my specialty is bioengineering, so I can't say I'm too familiar with mechanical engineering. However, based on my limited knowledge, I'd say it would be difficult to realize.
It's different from seaborne ships. If the materials aren't robust enough, the ship will fall apart the moment it tries to move.
(Impatiently blows at his red streak of hair)
What if we used steel for the keel?
I'm afraid that would make them too heavy. That's precisely why it's difficult to make wind-powered ones.
Then would you say making such a landship is near impossible?
No, I'm sure someone could come up with a design. Once you manage to work out the kinks, the actual construction shouldn't be too difficult.
Well, if we're talking about today's Iberia trapped in its own web... It wouldn't be much more difficult than making a traditional landship.
I see...
(Impatiently waves his hands)
Look, I'm terribly sorry, but shouldn't we, uh, talk business first?
For example, the whereabouts of my friend...
Dios mio, forgive me! I got carried away. Could you describe him for me?
That's crazy, there's no way!
There's no way he'd do something like that!
Calm down, Señor Elysium. I am simply stating the facts.
What you're saying is that a Rhodes Island operator colluded with bandits from the Salt Flat, both in stealing the corpses of the townspeople and arranging a rescue from his execution.
He never mentioned Rhodes Island during the interrogation... but the crimes are real.
Ahem. There must be some sort of misunderstanding here.
I regret to inform you that all the information we have points towards him being this criminal.
Mm-ph!
(Wriggles about)
I said, stop following me already.
Show Nene a little more respect, chaval.
I'm treating it the way I would treat any of you.
Which clearly isn't respectful enough. Nene's far more useful than you are.
I thought it was your emergency rations.
It's practically the boss's heart and soul! Who'd dare harm a scale on its head?
Besides, it's got far sharper senses than us out in the Flat. How else are we supposed to find anything under all that salt?
What are you looking for?
Hah, what else? Relics of the Golden Age. Sometimes high-tech curios, sometimes luxury items or whatever else. Good money either way.
But doesn't the Inquisition crack down on these sorts of excavations for Golden Age antiques?
They may not like it, but the caravans sneaking into Iberia sure do. We trade these trinkets for water and supplies. It beats begging, that's for sure.
So it's smuggling...
Don't give me that look. You think it's easy, living out here?
But you, chaval...
Every last one of the materials we got you for your alchemy, down to the smallest finbone, was bought by the blood, sweat, and tears of our crew.
...So think it over. If you know you can't do it, then don't waste our booty.
What are you suggesting?
Do a little hoo-hah during your alchemy, show the boss you failed, and give her a reason to back down from this madness.
(Whispers) Can't you just, I don't know, let me go?
Also, why not tell her that yourself?
Don't want her to think we're against the plan. We respect her... fear her.
Fear? Well, I suppose she is an amazing fighter.
There're better reasons to be afraid of her.
Juana stops in her tracks, as though she sensed something in the wind.
She looks behind her. She sees no trace of any living creature, besides her own footprints left on the desolate Salt Flat.
She breathes a sigh of relief and continues onwards. There is a giant skeleton in front of her, and below it, a dark shadow.
She steps into the shadow, and disappears.
There was a mutiny, back in the day, but only a few old bastards still remember it.
Someone said—in front of everyone—that she'd been working with the sea monsters, that she offered her entire crew up for freakish strength and longevity.
You mean the Seaborn?

Whatever they're called. At the time, the sea had only just retreated, and the monsters were still running wild. It was thanks to Captain Cruz and his crew risking life and limb that most of 'em were cleared out.
And Captain Cruz... They all died out in the Flat. Well, almost all. Juana was the only one to come back.
And when she did, she took over the entire fleet. Anyone who didn't wanna follow her ended up dying, one by one. On hunting trips or in Inquisition ambushes.
You said these were just rumors.
...Even Teach was but a lass when this all went down.
So you think she isn't actually trying to fix the compass to help everyone find a way out?
Mayhaps... Could be part of a much bigger plan. Nobody knows what she really wants.
Are you the only one who suspects her, Señor Rus?
...There's a few of us.
What about Señor Javier?
No, that boy has never doubted her for a second.
She's taken care of him so long, he sees her as a mother. She took him under her wing after his mother died.
Those Seaborn in the rumors... Have any of the sailors ever seen one?
Nope... Not a one.
Mmp...Mmph!
*Glub*—!
What's its problem?
Hah, looks like Nene found something.
Stow the sails, you scallywags! Drop anchor! We're setting down!
You heard him! Get digging!
Remember to bring your shovel and your sword, chaval. Don't even think about running. I'll have someone keep an eye on you.
...A ship's log?
You recognize that?
It's something mariners used to use to record what happened on their voyages.
So, you seen a lot of this sorta thing?
...Sí. My teacher taught me many things.
Must've had guts to do that.
So are you going to stand behind me and commentate all day, or...?
Think it was my idea, chaval? Bosun told me to keep a close eye on you.
I'm sure you can do that without literally breathing down my neck.
Hmph.
That sound... I know that sound...
Isidro looks around vigilantly, warned by the sound on the salt wind. Behind the sailor, he sees a familiar dent appear on the surface of the salt.
Out of the way! Saltfin incoming!
You got finshit for brains?!
Mreep... Mreep...
You shoved me aside for this?
You think it was one of them giant saltfins or something?
Shut up...
If it were, the whole ground'd be shaking. One of these small fry just leaves a little dent is all.
Besides, they're the same sorta critter as Nene. What's it gonna do, cuddle you to death?
What's that crazy puta even need from a guy as stupid as you, anyway?!
Mmph...
Mreep...
They're... the same species?
Mreep...
So there's more than one variety of saltfin out here?
Yep. About ten or so types, big and small.
Some of your sails are made from leather. Are they...
Saltfin hide. Also use it for tents.
And the meat you hang out to dry outside...
Saltfin meat.
Your weapons...
Saltfin bones.
And those guys are...
Mmph...
Mreep...
They ain't the same. Won't hurt 'em unless we really have to.
The crazy puta loves 'em to death. Once tried to haul some aboard to raise them, but a day away from their friends and they lose all the spirit. She had to give up that loco plan.
Then why's that one part of the crew?
Mm-ph.
Nene was abandoned by its mates. Couldn't keep up with 'em thanks to its weak body. Lucky for it, she went and picked it up.
Cheep...
Mm-ph!
They're leaving?
That's normal. They don't stay in one place for too long.
One after the other, the saltfins drill into the salt, speeding off into the distance.
Their tracks lace and intertwine, leaving the salt surface looking like a cracked mirror.
They fascinate Isidro. He climbs up on a jagged rock, hoping to get a better look at the where the cracks are headed from a higher vantage point.
He stands at its peak, and to his surprise, he sees an immense gash barreling towards them, swallowing up any salt reefs or skeletons in its path.
He realizes the saltfins weren't leaving. They were fleeing.
(Runs around anxiously)
No... That's definitely not normal.
You see something?!
Move! Quickly!
In an instant, the ground splits into a gaping maw, threatening to devour the two as they sprint away.
Cesc stumbles as he tries to flee, the collapsing earth grabbing at his ankle. He screams in terror.
Isidro grabs hold of his arm, but he soon finds out he is powerless against nature's brute strength.
Just as the two are about to be swallowed whole, the collapse miraculously stops. The sailor's lower leg is wedged tight between the jagged earth, now nothing more than a bloody, mangled mess.
The hell's going on over there?!
The salt split open! I think someone fell in!
Go have a look then, quick!
I'll be going now.
Get back here, you capullo! Don't even think about running!
Hey... Don't leave me here by myself...
Mm... hmph...
Your crewmates will be over soon. You'll be fine.
Wh... Where are you going?
(Nips at Isidro's pants)
Not sure. Somewhere you can't find me.
...N—No... You can't go.
Bosun's... orders... I... have to keep an eye on you.
*Glub*—!
I haven't been here to see you for so long...
All is going according to plan.
Yes, we've taken a few risks, but they were worth it.
Soon we will set our sails once more, and return to the ocean's embrace.
Are you happy for me?
The sound of metal scraping against stone echoes out from the cave, followed by a slender Seaborn slowly slithering its way out, its form warped and twisted.
Each of its tentacles is wrapped around a weapon. As their points and edges drag across the stone walls, they keen and wail.
It slowly approaches the saltfin meat, and slowly eats away at it, its eyes fixed on Juana the entire time.
She meets its gaze, quietly waiting for the Seaborn's reply.
But in her heart, she knows that it is no more than a beast, a beast without a speck of intellect or humanity.
We will leave this place, this desert of salt. No matter the price.
Juana turns and exits the cave, leaving behind only the Seaborn, trashing around like a caged animal now that it has finished its meal.
Rocks fall from the ceiling until finally, it ceases its struggle for a way out, slowly calming down in the darkness.
Dios mio... What happened here? Did that boy do this?!
N—No, it was my fault... B—But he ran away...
Forget him! You lot come over and help pull him out.
AHHHH! MY LEG!
Bosun, his leg...
Speak up!
I—I don't think we can save it! We need to call the surgeon!
When's the last time we had a surgeon...
But only a surgeon'd know how to cut it off right! Else he'll bleed to death!
I don't wanna die here...
Save me, bosun! Save me!
Cesc... I...
I'm here. I know what to do.
Chaval...
He'll live. I guarantee it.
Good. Get over here.
Bite down on the hilt. Hard.
Grkh...
This'll sting a little. Just bear with it.
GKKHHH—!
Thorns!
How could they do you dirty like this on your own wanted poster? What's with that picture?
...Why are you so quick to concede that it's Thorns now? Weren't you just insisting it was all a mistaken identity?
What if those bandits forced him into it? Or maybe he was framed? He's not much of a talker. I can definitely see him putting his foot in his mouth while explaining himself.
If he is the upstanding young man you say he is, then surely he's dead by now, killed by either the bandits or by the dangers of the Salt Flat.
No... What exactly would they do to him?
They like to... No, you would be happier not knowing.
We've done all we can to keep them away from town, but they've still managed to abduct some of our best and brightest.
Their leader is a vile monster, able to corrupt the hearts of others.
Oh...
Time to start cooking... Hmm? Who touched my kitchen?
(Cautious sniff)
That smell... It's coming from the pot over there...
Stewed fin hearts? This is Señora Juana's specialty.
But they went bad ages ago. Better put that fire out.
During the Profound Silence, most of Aarón was submerged under the sea. Only this small part atop the hill avoided that grim fate, but it too shortly fell to chaos as the harvest failed.
The pirates sailed in before the seawater retreated. They claimed they would bring order to Aarón.
But in truth, they only wished to burn, kill and maim, forcing the townsfolk to surrender to their reign of terror.
Everything they saw was put to the torch...
Fire! HELP! FIRE! The pot blew up!
Mierda! Why's there a fire here?!
Javier, you dried-out piece of finshit! How many times do I have to tell you NOT to let this useless cockeyed bastard in the kitchen until he gets some glasses?!
Ohh... It was fin oil... Sorry, was all blurry to me!
Out of my sight, dumbass!
—Where the hell's Javier? Get over here and put out this fire already!
Quick, cover it with salt!
Ahh! My clothes! I just sewed up that finleather coat!
...Mmm... Roast finleather smells... pretty good...

Even if he is lucky enough to be alive, the bandits themselves live harsh lives in the wastes. Surely they wouldn't treat their captives well. He's most likely dying of hunger and thirst at this very moment.
Are you alright, Elysium?
I'm fine... Señor Calificador, please, continue.
Needless to say, we can only pray he does nothing to incur their wrath.
Their punishments are as barbaric as they are capricious. They'll slit open bellies or chop off hands and feet over the slightest offense...
To think they'd twist the law like that...
S—Slit open bellies or chop off hands and feet, you say...?
AHHH—HHHHH!
Hah... Hah...
Get some rum! Give him a swig!
Rus! Rus! How's my leg...?
Alright... Lie down, and stay calm...
Your leg's still out in the Flat. It won't hurt any longer.
We've been looking after you here, Cesc. Don't worry, the surgery was a success. You won't be dying today.
Oh... Oh...
I just cut his leg off. I can't call it surgery.
He's alive. That's surgery enough for us.
Calm down, Elysium. There's nothing to suggest that these bandits have killed him.
I don't want to think too much about the possibility, but... where would we even go to find out?
Weedy does not answer. In the distance, several corpses hang from a pole outside of town, swinging in the wind.
A fowl lands atop it, feasting on the carrion.
Oh, you two are guests of the Inquisition.
Those were bandits caught by the Alguacil. They were hanged, all according to the teachings.
Their bodies will serve as a warning to others of their kind.
Dead if you stick with the bandits, dead if you come back looking like a bandit...
Thorns...
Hey, joven, you still want that paper you're holding?
Nah, I don't need it anymore. Here, take it.
Gracias. Finally found something to stick my bed together with.
Hmm? Isn't this...
What, have you seen him before?
Of course I have. I even sheltered him for a night.
The fleet's heading out into the flat to hunt saltfins in a few days, and I'd like the boy to come along.
We've lost a lot of men this month, and our surgeon and navigator sacrificed themselves to get us out of the Inquisition's trap. I think he can fill in for their posts.
That's how you plan to fix our manpower problem?
I only let him help on the salvage ship because of Teach's guarantee, but did he not almost run away today?
And now you're telling me you want him to join you on a finship.
Señora... I know he's a precious, precious alchemist, but he's great with a sword too and knows his medicine. He'll be real handy during a hunt.
You might as well open the door and let him leave. He'll find no shortage of opportunities to escape out there.
I'm a pirate, Rus. We're not the most kind-hearted sort.
Please, señora, we've lost way too many men in these last few tussles with the Inquisition.
You're not the only one in this fleet who needs him... We all do.
He's far from a good little boy, you know. In fact, he's rather naughty. How will you ensure he does as he's told?
He'd never met Pascuala before, but he still saved her sorry behind when the Inquisition was chasing her down, and saw her safely to town. Didn't ask for a reward, neither.
He could've left Cesc to die, but he didn't. Chose to come back and help us save him.
He... He's a good kid. An honest kid.
As long as we show him how much we need him, he won't just sit back and watch us suffer.
Any other news from the crew?
Nothing yet... Nothing about our food supplies for the next month, at least.
*Sigh*...
Fine, he can go with you.
So the boy was your friend, then?
A fine young man. Such a shame what happened... And in the prime of his youth...
Alright, alright, fine!
I'm going to go find him myself!
Elysium? Elysium!
Weedy, Thorns is definitely out there, waiting for us to save him!
Here, a communicator. You stay in town, I'll head out into the Salt Flat.
Huh... Wha...?
If anything happens here, contact me! We'll keep in touch—
Take care—
ELYSIUM!
Wait for me, Thorns! I'm coming!
Why do you kids never let me finish?
You were trying to say...
It's a shame a fine young man like that would come back to Iberia just to learn a bit of swordplay.
Wouldn't it be better to live out the rest of your days in some other country?
This bread is... burnt.
Should still be edible, at least.
That bunch of useless pricks... I'll cave their skulls in one day, mark my words.
Lucky we didn't lose too much.
(*chews*)
Why're you snacking on a lump of charcoal?
Because I'm hungry.
Toss that out and come with me.
